one_line_takeaway: AFSL is a three-part feature-similarity training objective that pulls adversarial examples back to their true class while pushing real and fake features apart, restoring deepfake-detector robust AUC from ~1% to ~80% under 8/255 attacks and edging out TRADES across most white-box and black-box attacks on the FF++ family.
key_finding: Undefended detectors collapse almost completely under adversarial attack: RealForensics scores 91.5% average clean AUC but only ~1.0% under an 8/255 attack (Table 2). AFSL substantially restores robustness while largely preserving clean accuracy. On FF++ cross-manipulation (Table 2), RealForensics+AFSL reaches 80.8% average robust AUC vs 76.7% for TRADES and 73.5% for AT, with clean AUC of 89.7% (vs 91.5% undefended). Across attack types (Table 3), RealForensics+AFSL leads on every attack: PGD10 80.7 vs 76.7 (TRADES), CW2 82.8 vs 78.1, StatAttack 73.9 vs 67.2, Universal 74.7 vs 69.3, RBB 87.5 vs 82.4. Gains hold on unseen datasets (Table 4) and on frame-based detectors (Table 5, XceptionNet AFSL PGD10 accuracy 81.7 vs TRADES 80.8). The ablation (Table 6) shows the adversarial similarity loss is essential (S1 with classification loss only = 1.0% AUC; adding Lasl jumps to ~79-81%; adding Lsrl adds ~2%). AFSL also improves robustness to seven common non-adversarial distortions (Fig. 2).
core_contribution: The paper proposes Adversarial Feature Similarity Learning (AFSL), a training objective for making deepfake video detectors robust to adversarial perturbations. AFSL adds three loss terms to the detector's training: (1) a deepfake classification loss using logit-adjusted binary cross-entropy to handle real/fake class imbalance; (2) an adversarial similarity loss that maximizes cosine similarity between the features of a clean sample and its PGD-generated adversarial counterpart, pulling perturbed examples back toward their true class; and (3) a similarity regularization loss that minimizes cosine similarity between paired real and fake samples to keep the two classes separated and preserve clean accuracy. The final objective is L = Ldcl + b1*Lasl + b2*Lsrl with b1=1 and b2=0.1. The authors position AFSL against TRADES, arguing that their feature-similarity regularization captures finer real-vs-fake differences than TRADES' clean-adversarial distance regularization. AFSL is a drop-in objective that can be attached to existing detectors (RealForensics, XceptionNet, MesoNet are used).
threat_model: Adversary applies small, imperceptible perturbations (bounded by L-infinity = 8/255) to deepfake videos to make a detector misclassify them, evading detection. The paper evaluates both white-box attacks where the adversary has full model access (PGD10, CW2, the Robust White-box Attack RWA from Adversarial Deepfakes, StatAttack/SA statistical-consistency attack, and a Universal attack Ul) and a black-box setting (RBB, Robust Black-Box attack, with perturbations transferred from a separate ResNet3D-based model without access to the target's parameters). Defender trains the detector with AFSL using PGD5 during training; attacks at test time use PGD10.
limitations: Margins over TRADES are modest (often ~2-4 AUC points) and not uniform: on DeeperForensics under the Universal attack, standalone AFSL (83.5) trails TRADES (85.6) and RealForensics+AFSL only ties it (85.6), and on FaceShifter under StatAttack AFSL (65.1) is below TRADES (67.3) (Table 4). Baseline numbers are reproduced from official code and the authors note they differ from the original papers due to version/hyperparameter differences and absence of supplementary data, complicating direct comparison. Evaluation is confined to the FF++ family (FF++, FaceShifter, DeeperForensics) so generalization to other generators/datasets is untested. Attacks are standard (PGD/CW/StatAttack/Universal/RBB) rather than adaptive attacks designed against the AFSL objective itself, and the perturbation budget is fixed at 8/255. The method depends on strong self-supervised RealForensics/LRW pretraining for the sequence model.

datasets_used: FaceForensics++ (FF++), c23 mildly compressed, 1,000 real + 4,000 deepfake videos, four manipulation types DF/FS/F2F/NT
FaceShifter (unseen face-swap dataset on FF++ real videos)
Lip Reading in the Wild (LRW) - used only for RealForensics self-supervised pretraining
quantitative_results: RealForensics clean average AUC 91.5% drops to 1.0% under 8/255 attack (Table 2)
FF++ average robust AUC (Table 2): RealForensics+AFSL 80.8, standalone AFSL 78.6, TRADES 76.7, AT 73.5; clean RealForensics+AFSL 89.7
Per-attack average AUC for RealForensics+AFSL (Table 3): No Attack 89.8, PGD10 80.7, RWA 81.3, CW2 82.8, StatAttack 73.9, Universal 74.7, RBB 87.5 - highest in every column
TRADES per-attack (Table 3): PGD10 76.7, RWA 76.1, CW2 78.1, StatAttack 67.2, Universal 69.3, RBB 82.4
DeeperForensics RealForensics+AFSL (Table 4): No Attack 92.9, PGD10 83.6, CW2 83.5, RBB 89.1
Frame-level accuracy, XceptionNet (Table 5): AFSL PGD10 81.7 / CW2 82.9 / RWA 80.6 / SA 77.5 / RBB 85.7 vs TRADES 80.8 / 79.4 / 77.2 / 76.8 / 84.7 vs AT 76.0 / 72.7 / 73.1 / 58.9 / 80.1
MesoNet (Table 5): AFSL PGD10 79.5 vs TRADES 78.2 vs AT 74.3
Ablation PGD10 robust AUC on DeeperForensics/FaceShifter (Table 6): S1 Ldcl only 1.0/1.0; S2 +Lasl 81.3/79.1; S3 +Lsrl 83.6/81.7
Loss weights b1=1, b2=0.1; adversarial budget L-inf=8/255; PGD5 for training, PGD10 for testing
baselines_compared: Vanilla Adversarial Training (Madry et al., AT)
TRADES (Zhang et al.) - stated state-of-the-art baseline
Undefended detectors: RealForensics, LipForensics, FTCN, Patch-based, XceptionNet, MesoNet
Adversarial Deepfakes (Hussain et al.) as an undefended/attack reference in frame-level Table 5
